faroe-islands.summary: The recovered Faroe Islands record corresponds to Nólsoyar Orkufelag's 220 kW stand-alone wind-to-heat microgrid on Nólsoy, not the multi-island SEV electric system. A reused Wind World induction-generator turbine supplies resistive heating loads and was designed to buffer variable wind as hot water. The most detailed operating update found, from June 2020, says the volunteer-run project was still manually controlled, turned only intermittently, and served eight houses rather than the 23-house first phase or roughly 35-house technical potential. No reviewed source supports the recovered Schneider Electric attribution.
faroe-islands.status: Installed in 2012 but only intermittently operating as of the latest detailed local update in June 2020, when eight houses were connected and control remained manual. The Faroese regulator still publishes the project's 2011 permit, and a commercial wind database labeled the turbine operational in May 2026, but neither establishes recent output or confirms that the planned district-heating tank and full automation were completed. A separate 2025 Nólsoy Energy virtual-power-plant pilot uses household solar, heat pumps, and EV batteries; it is not evidence that the older wind-heating system was upgraded.

organizations.name: Nólsoyar Orkufelag
organizations.role: Community company that applied for the permit, owns the turbine, and was still developing and manually operating the heating project with volunteer labor in 2020.

organizations.name: Wind World
organizations.role: Original manufacturer of the reused W2500 wind turbine installed for the project.

organizations.name: Aalborg University
organizations.role: Affiliation of the researchers who documented the self-excited induction-generator controls and modeled thermal-storage performance.

specifications.label: Wind turbine
specifications.value: One Wind World W2500 rated 220 kW, with a 25 m rotor and 30 m tower; three-phase 690 V, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction generator

specifications.label: Heating reach
specifications.value: Wind and household-demand measurements indicated enough annual energy for about 35 houses; the permit described 23 nearby homes in the first district-heating phase, while only eight were connected in June 2020

specifications.label: Central hot-water storage
specifications.value: The 2011 permit described a planned 48 m³ tank; the 2020 retrospective described the project design as 69 m³. Public evidence reviewed does not establish which size, if either, was completed.

specifications.label: Modeled annual wind energy
specifications.value: Approximately 870 MWh/year based on three years of wind data; this was a pre-operating estimate, not verified annual production

specifications.label: Modeled household thermal storage
specifications.value: 100 kWh per house, equivalent to about 2,000 L of water cycled from 55°C to 95°C, produced an estimated 85% wind-heat share with about 15% curtailment and 15% backup heat in simulation

specifications.label: Backup heat in permitted design
specifications.value: Approximately 100 kW gas-oil boiler, with the permit requiring any installation to remain below 120 kW unless reapproved

equipment.detail: A reused early-1990s, stall-regulated Danish turbine with a 220 kW squirrel-cage induction generator, repurposed after roughly two decades of operation in Denmark.

The research configuration used an Arduino-class microcontroller to switch resistive heater banks for frequency control and capacitor banks for reactive power and voltage control. The paper reports improving the adjustment interval from about 260 ms to 62 ms and implementing PID loops; a commercial product model is not named.

Electric immersion heaters convert otherwise variable stand-alone wind output to hot-water heat. The permit contemplated a central tank, district-heating pipes, smaller household tanks, and an oil-fired backup boiler; completion of the full configuration is not verified.

technicalDetails.detail: The project deliberately operated the turbine as a self-excited, stand-alone electrical microgrid because connecting it to the local utility grid proved difficult. It is an electricity-to-heat system, not Nólsoy's normal electric supply, which remains a separate utility network.

Frequency is regulated by continually matching switched heater load to wind generation; voltage is regulated by switched capacitors supplying the induction generator's magnetizing reactive power. High-wind self-excitation during startup was a documented control challenge.

The permitted design anticipated a 23-home first phase and later public buildings and additional homes. In 2020, the operator said only eight homes were connected, operation was manual and intermittent, technical work remained, and volunteers were completing it in their spare time.

The 85% wind-heat share, 15% curtailment, 15% backup requirement, and 870 MWh/year are model results. No public measured annual production, fuel savings, availability, or post-2020 connection count was located. The May 2026 commercial database status should therefore not be treated as proof of reliable operation.

Nólsoy Energy introduced a community virtual-power-plant pilot in 2025 using household solar, heat pumps, bidirectional EV charging, and grid transactions. The announcement does not connect that pilot to the W2500 wind-heating microgrid, so its assets are not added to this project's capacity.

sources.title: Environmental permit for Sp/F Nólsoyar Orkufelag's wind turbine
sources.publisher: Umhvørvisstovan (Environment Agency of the Faroe Islands)

sources.title: Faroe Islands Wind-Powered Space Heating Microgrid Using Self-Excited 220-kW Induction Generator
sources.publisher: IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Energy / Aalborg University

flacq.summary: The Flacq record is the grid-connected, behind-the-meter hybrid-energy system at the Flacq Coeur de Ville shopping center and Super U supermarket in Mauritius. DHYBRID's current project page describes 1.2 MWp of PV, 2,000 kVA of diesel generation, electrical storage of undisclosed size, chilled thermal storage, and its Universal Power Platform. Solar is self-consumed rather than exported in the current operating description, while central cooling and refrigeration create ice or cold storage from surplus daytime solar for use at night.
flacq.status: Operating commercial installation. The shopping center opened in June 2015, the official 1.146 MW PV application received a conditional permit in August 2015, and later accounts place formal plant inauguration in 2017. A 2023 environmental-award report still identified Super U Flacq's 1.146 MW PV system in use. DHYBRID continues to publish the installation as a reference project, but no audited post-commissioning production, diesel savings, battery availability, or storage capacity was located.

organizations.name: Super U Flacq
organizations.role: Customer, supermarket anchor, and principal on-site energy user.

organizations.name: Flacq Coeur de Ville / Tsin Cepage Flacq Property Ltd
organizations.role: Shopping-center host and property entity associated with the Super U Flacq site.

organizations.name: DHYBRID Power Systems
organizations.role: Turnkey EPC, system integrator, and supplier of the plant-wide hybrid controls; its current reference says installation took eight weeks.

organizations.name: Central Electricity Board
organizations.role: Mauritius electric utility that reviewed and conditionally permitted the 1.146 MW self-consumption/distributed-generation application and supplies the balance and backup from the grid.

specifications.label: Solar PV rating
specifications.value: 1.146 MWp in the government permit and commissioning account; rounded to 1.2 MWp in the current DHYBRID reference

specifications.label: PV module count
specifications.value: Sources differ by phase and rounding: 4,504 at the June 2015 shopping-center opening, 4,584 in the 2017 plant-inauguration report, and approximately 4,700 on DHYBRID's current project page

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: 2,000 kVA total; the 2015 account specifies two 1,000 kVA gensets

specifications.label: PV inverters
specifications.value: 70 units in DHYBRID's current as-represented configuration; an earlier project account described 75, so this likely reflects a design/as-built change or later consolidation

specifications.label: Vendor-reported annual solar generation
specifications.value: 1,800 MWh/year on the current DHYBRID reference; the 2017 inauguration article reported 1,179 MWh/year. Neither figure was independently audited in the sources reviewed.

specifications.label: Vendor-reported annual fuel and emissions savings
specifications.value: DHYBRID's current page lists 486,000 L of diesel and 1.134 million kg of CO2 per year in its scope table, while its narrative rounds fuel savings to 500,000 L/year

specifications.label: Project cost reported at inauguration
specifications.value: Approximately MUR 80 million, reported as about €2 million in 2017

equipment.detail: Coordinates PV, utility-grid supply, diesel backup, electrical storage, and chilled thermal storage. Earlier project-development reporting used DHYBRID's older HyGRID controller name.
Excess daytime PV is stored as ice or cold for the central air-conditioning and food-refrigeration systems and used at night; tank size, thermal capacity, and equipment vendor are not disclosed.
DHYBRID lists an energy-storage system and says storage plus diesel can support full-load autonomy, but it does not disclose chemistry, manufacturer, power rating, or energy capacity.
The reviewed public sources disclose counts and aggregate ratings but not PV-module, inverter, or genset manufacturers and models.

technicalDetails.detail: This is a behind-the-meter hybrid at a grid-connected shopping center, not an off-grid village microgrid. The CEB told Parliament that PV would cover only about 30% of site requirements and that the utility would supply the balance and backup.

The July 2014 application sought self-consumption and supply to the grid under the medium-scale distributed-generation/net-metering scheme. DHYBRID's current operating description says all self-generated energy is consumed on site with no export. The page preserves this as a dated application-versus-current-configuration distinction.

The UPP uses the refrigeration and central-air-conditioning plant as a controllable energy sink, shifting solar energy into nighttime cooling via ice/cold storage rather than relying only on electrical batteries.

The 1.146 MW government/commissioning value is the more precise permitted rating; 1.2 MW is a rounded commercial description. Likewise, module and inverter counts differ across planning, opening, inauguration, and current vendor sources and should not be combined as separate equipment additions.

A 2023 report confirms the PV installation remained part of Super U Flacq's operating energy-management program. Public sources do not provide current annual energy mix, measured availability, verified lifetime diesel savings, export data, or electrical/thermal storage ratings.

flores-island-microgrid.summary: Flores is an isolated island power system in the Portuguese Azores, not a single fixed-generation package. In 2024 it combined the 1.5 MW Ribeira de Além Fazenda hydro plant, the end-of-life 600 kW Boca da Vereda wind farm, diesel generation at the Lajes thermal station, and a historically installed ABB PowerStore flywheel. EDA Renováveis reports that hydro supplied 44.2% and wind 1.5% of island electricity in 2024, for a 45.7% renewable share; diesel supplied the remaining approximately 54.3%. The two old 300 kW wind turbines were dismantled for replacement by one 900 kW machine, but the latest March 2026 evidence documents equipment logistics rather than completed commissioning.
flores-island-microgrid.status: Operating isolated utility system, with hydro and diesel in service and wind repowering still unresolved. EDA Renováveis began dismantling the two old wind turbines in late 2024 and originally planned full operation of the 900 kW replacement by year-end 2025. RTP/Lusa reported in March 2026 that transport of the replacement towers, nacelles, and blades had been completed for the three-island repowering program, but did not say the Flores turbine was commissioned. The current availability of the 2005-era ABB flywheel is not disclosed in EDA's 2024 report.

organizations.name: EDA — Electricidade dos Açores
organizations.role: Island electric utility and operator of the diesel thermal station, grid, and planned fast-response battery reserve.

organizations.name: EDA Renováveis
organizations.role: Owner/operator of the Flores hydro and wind assets and developer of the current wind repowering and planned solar expansion.

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Supplier of the historic PowerStore flywheel grid-stabilization system used to integrate wind on the isolated grid.

organizations.name: Enercon
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the retired E-30 turbines and the planned E-44 replacement turbine in the repowering program.

organizations.name: Transinsular
organizations.role: Marine and inter-island logistics provider for the wind-turbine repowering equipment delivered in the 2026 update.

specifications.label: Current hydro nameplate
specifications.value: 1.5 MW for Ribeira de Além Fazenda in EDA Renováveis' 2024 portfolio table

specifications.label: 2024 hydro output and mix
specifications.value: 5.106 GWh, down about 4% from 2023 because of lower precipitation; 44.2% of Flores electricity

specifications.label: Retired wind configuration
specifications.value: Two Enercon E-30 turbines at 300 kW each, 600 kW total, installed in 2002; 44 m hub height and 30 m rotor diameter

specifications.label: 2024 wind output and mix
specifications.value: 0.17 GWh and 1.5% of Flores electricity while the old machines were reaching end of life and being removed

specifications.label: 2024 renewable and diesel mix
specifications.value: 45.7% renewable: 44.2% hydro plus 1.5% wind. The remaining approximately 54.3% is inferred diesel generation because EDA identifies gas oil as the thermal fuel on Flores.

specifications.label: Wind repowering target
specifications.value: One Enercon E-44 rated 900 kW, with 55 m hub height and 44 m rotor diameter; medium-voltage collection replaces the old low-voltage arrangement

specifications.label: Historic flywheel
specifications.value: ABB PowerStore rated 500 kW with about 60 seconds of full-power duration, commonly reported as approximately 5 kWh / 18 MW-seconds; commissioned in 2005

equipment.detail: The remodeled Além Fazenda plant documents two 528 kW horizontal-axis Francis turbines, each coupled to a 640 kVA three-phase synchronous generator. EDA's current portfolio gives 1.5 MW total plant nameplate; the public plant page does not reconcile the additional rating with the two new units.
Two 300 kW direct-drive turbines installed in 2002 and dismantled beginning in late 2024 after reaching end of useful life.
One 900 kW turbine planned for Boca da Vereda. Components were included in the three-island logistics campaign completed by March 2026, but a commissioning notice for Flores was not located.

Fast-response flywheel/inverter system that absorbs or injects real power and supplies voltage/frequency support, allowing wind operation with less need to keep diesel generation online. Current availability was not established.

The isolated grid uses gas-oil-fired generating sets at the Lajes thermal station. Current aggregate nameplate, unit count, manufacturers, and models were not disclosed in the reviewed current sources; the recovered 600 kW diesel figure should not be treated as the present plant total.

technicalDetails.detail: The hydro intake, headpond, auxiliaries, and generating groups were fully automated in the 2015 remodel. The plant can operate unattended and is remotely controlled from the Lajes thermal station.

ABB described wind-induced voltage and frequency fluctuations, blackouts, and curtailment before PowerStore. Its fast injection/absorption let the utility smooth wind and at times operate without an online diesel set; this is a historical operating account, not a statement of current availability.

EDA's 2024 report planned new foundations, platforms, underground medium-voltage connections, and switchgear, with full service targeted for the end of 2025. A March 2026 logistics update confirms delivery work, implying schedule slippage or at least incomplete public closeout; it does not establish commercial operation.

EDA documents future fast-response BESS deployment on Flores and a planned Flores PV project, but the reviewed sources do not establish commissioning, capacity, vendor, or model. These are excluded from installed capacity.

The separate Ribeira Grande hydro project on Flores, estimated around 1.2 MW in EDA's 2024 accounts, remained under geological and routing reevaluation. It is not part of current installed hydro capacity.

The 2024 mix is measured and current for that year. Public evidence reviewed does not provide 2025 or 2026 island generation mix, current thermal nameplate, a current PowerStore status, or proof that the 900 kW turbine, BESS, or PV project entered service.

fort-collins-microgrid.summary: The Fort Collins entry is the municipal FortZED Renewable and Distributed Systems Integration demonstration, not a military installation and not one co-located generator plant. From 2008 through 2013, Fort Collins Utilities aggregated PV, methane and natural-gas CHP, conventional generators, microturbines, fuel cells, thermal storage, controllable loads, and vehicle-to-grid assets at roughly five participant locations on two distribution feeders. A second phase intentionally islanded a smaller microgrid linking Colorado State University's Engines and Energy Conversion Laboratory with the Northside Aztlan Community Center.
fort-collins-microgrid.status: Demonstration completed and no longer operating as an integrated project. City material says Phase I achieved a 20–30% peak-demand reduction on the two feeders and Phase II demonstrated the EECL–Northside Aztlan microgrid with cybersecurity safeguards. A 2020 NREL retrospective says the project was operational in 2012 but was disbanded in 2017 because of regulatory factors, historic distribution practices, and the natural end of the roughly ten-year collaboration. Individual participant assets may have continued independently, but the central FortZED dispatch system should not be represented as current.

organizations.name: City of Fort Collins / Fort Collins Utilities
organizations.role: Project lead, prime contractor, electric utility, feeder operator, and host of municipal generation, load-control, thermal-storage, and vehicle-to-grid assets.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy / National Energy Technology Laboratory
organizations.role: Federal RDSI program sponsor and project-management organization; DOE provided about $6.696 million of the $11.798 million project value.

organizations.name: Spirae
organizations.role: Technology partner for the smart-grid platform, distributed-resource/power-management system, asset interfaces, and integration at the InteGrid laboratory.

organizations.name: Colorado State University / InteGrid Laboratory
organizations.role: Demonstration-site operator and R&D partner for controls, PHEV/V2G, simulation, mixed distributed generation, and the Phase II intentional-islanding microgrid.

organizations.name: New Belgium Brewing
organizations.role: Major commercial demonstration site for PV, process-methane CHP, thermal storage, smart metering, and demand response.

organizations.name: Larimer County
organizations.role: Participant and host of parking-garage PV and controllable loads.

organizations.name: Advanced Energy, Woodward, Eaton, Brendle Group, and Caterpillar
organizations.role: Technology partners respectively associated with PV inverters, mixed-fuel and generator controls, switchgear/power components, demand-management analysis, and distributed generation.

specifications.label: Resources under management
specifications.value: NREL's 2020 retrospective reports 4 MW of generation plus 760 kW of demand-side/load-shed resources; DOE's project page describes more than 3.5 MW of aggregated DER across approximately five locations

specifications.label: 2008 design inventory
specifications.value: 3,988.7 kW across generation, thermal storage, vehicle-to-grid, and load-shedding line items; this early design total is not a current plant nameplate and not every item is independently confirmed as commissioned

specifications.label: Peak-load objective and outcome
specifications.value: Minimum objective 15%, target 20–30%; city and NREL closeout material report 20–30% peak-demand reduction on the two feeders

specifications.label: Project duration
specifications.value: October 1, 2008 through September 30, 2013 for the DOE project; FortZED collaboration later disbanded in 2017

specifications.label: Project value
specifications.value: $11,797,949 total: $6,695,880 DOE share and $5,102,069 non-DOE share

specifications.label: 2008 New Belgium design resources
specifications.value: 200 kW new PV; 292 kW Gauscor methane CHP; 650 kW Cat 3508C methane CHP; 135 kW thermal storage; 160 kW load shedding

specifications.label: 2008 InteGrid laboratory design resources
specifications.value: Two 80 kW Onan natural-gas gensets, one 300 kW Cat natural-gas genset, 80 kW Ingersoll Rand and 80 kW Bowman microturbines, 100 kW wind-turbine simulator, and two Plug Power fuel cells totaling 10 kW

equipment.detail: Central platform modeled and monitored feeder and asset state, issued dispatch commands, tracked response, and interfaced to local controllers and building systems. NREL credits standardized central control with automatically dispatching multiple owners' DER for demand reduction.
Specified for the 200 kW new PV array in the 2008 design inventory; model and final as-built count are not stated.
The design listed a 292 kW Gauscor unit with Woodward fuel-blending controls and Eaton switchgear plus a 650 kW Cat 3508C, both using brewery process methane.

equipment.manufacturer: Cummins/Onan, Caterpillar, Ingersoll Rand, Bowman, and Plug Power
equipment.detail: The 2008 test-bed design included natural-gas gensets, two makes of microturbine, a Cummins diesel-driven induction-generator wind simulator, and two fuel cells. These laboratory devices supported integration testing and should not be described as renewable generation deployed across the city.
Two city-fleet PHEVs were listed at 10 kW combined in the early design. Public closeout sources reviewed do not give battery size or verified delivered V2G energy.

Eaton supplied or developed switchgear/power components and Woodward contributed power management and mixed-fuel controls across project sites; individual final device models are not consistently disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: Phase I coordinated assets at municipal facilities, New Belgium, CSU, Larimer County, and the InteGrid laboratory over two utility feeders. The aggregate resource figure therefore combines generation, storage-like thermal loads, and demand response owned by several parties rather than describing one fenced microgrid plant.

The later phase demonstrated an intentionally islanded microgrid comprising CSU's Engines and Energy Conversion Laboratory and the Northside Aztlan Center, including advanced cybersecurity safeguards for control communications.

DOE lists PV, microturbines, dual-fuel CHP using process methane, reciprocating engines, backup generators, a wind simulator, V2G plug-in hybrids, fuel cells, HVAC/process load shedding, thermal storage, and efficiency upgrades. The mix was designed to test dispatch and verification, not maximize renewable-energy percentage.

The project met its peak-reduction demonstration objective and advanced multi-building control, but did not achieve the original vision of a net-zero-energy district before the collaboration ended. NREL identifies stakeholder cooperation, regulatory structure, and existing utility practices as important lessons.

The detailed equipment list is a 2008 design snapshot, while later DOE, city, and NREL sources validate aggregate scale and outcomes. No current integrated FortZED controller operation or comprehensive inventory of surviving participant equipment was located; legacy solar, fuel-cell, gas/diesel, and thermal figures should not be presented as a current energy mix.

fire-station-24-charlotte-nc-microgrid.summary: Duke Energy's McAlpine Creek single-customer microgrid islands Charlotte Fire Station 24 using a 50 kW photovoltaic array, a BYD lithium-iron-phosphate battery, utility-owned switching and controls, and the station's pre-existing diesel generator. McAlpine has served as a Duke test site since 2006; storage was installed in late 2012, integration began in 2013, and the microgrid entered service in July 2015. Primary sources disagree on whether the battery power rating is 200 kW/200 kVA or 240 kW, but consistently report 500 kWh of energy. A later 30 kW Eos battery was connected to the same solar array by 2020; its energy capacity and present role are not published.
fire-station-24-charlotte-nc-microgrid.status: Operational pilot in Duke and SEL's latest project-specific descriptions, with the core system in service since July 2015. It completed automatic storm transfers in April and September 2016 and later secondary reporting says it rode through additional outages. Duke's 2020 integrated resource plan is the latest dated primary source found that describes the site's configuration, so current 2026 availability, measured reliability, battery state of health, and whether the added Eos unit remains active are unverified.

organizations.name: Duke Energy
organizations.role: Owner, operator, and maintainer of the solar, battery, distribution, control, and communications equipment at the McAlpine Creek test site.

organizations.name: Charlotte Fire Department / City of Charlotte
organizations.role: Host and critical-load customer at Fire Station 24; the station's existing diesel generator was incorporated as an independent backup source.

organizations.name: Schweitzer Engineering Laboratories
organizations.role: Supplied the real-time automation controller, advanced recloser control, communications, and microgrid control engineering.

organizations.name: BYD
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the original 500 kWh lithium-iron-phosphate battery system.

organizations.name: Satcon
organizations.role: Manufacturer of the photovoltaic inverter identified in the technical implementation paper.

organizations.name: Eos Energy Storage
organizations.role: Supplier of a separate 30 kW battery added at the test site by 2020; the reviewed source does not disclose its energy capacity.

organizations.name: Electric Power Research Institute
organizations.role: Documented and evaluated the integrated utility microgrid demonstration.

specifications.label: Solar generation
specifications.value: 50 kW photovoltaic array

specifications.label: Original battery
specifications.value: BYD lithium-iron-phosphate, consistently reported as 500 kWh; Duke, EPRI, and the 2020 IRP use 200 kW or 200 kVA, while SEL's engineering paper and current case page use 240 kW

specifications.label: Later battery addition
specifications.value: 30 kW Eos battery connected to the same 50 kW photovoltaic resource by 2020; chemistry, energy capacity, and commissioning date are not disclosed in the reviewed IRP

specifications.label: Island electrical service
specifications.value: 480 V, 60 Hz microgrid serving the fire station behind the point-of-common-coupling isolation device

specifications.label: Claimed support duration
specifications.value: Duke estimated that battery and solar could support the station for as long as 36 hours under favorable load and solar conditions; this was an expectation, not a measured full-duration result

equipment.detail: Modular lithium-iron-phosphate system assembled from roughly 1,700 small-format batteries, rated 500 kWh and variously described as 200 kW/200 kVA or 240 kW. The discrepancy likely reflects different nominal and inverter/test ratings, but the sources do not explicitly reconcile it.
The existing inverter firmware was modified so the solar system could operate as a voltage source during an island rather than only follow the utility grid.
Coordinates island detection, source dispatch, synchronization, and transition using DNP3 over Ethernet and hard/fiber signaling including MIRRORED BITS communications.
Controls the isolation recloser and supports both manual and automatic grid-to-island and island-to-grid transitions.
The fire station already had a diesel generator before microgrid integration. Its manufacturer, model, fuel storage, and kW rating were not published in the reviewed primary sources.
A separate 30 kW unit was added to study another storage technology. Public project documentation reviewed does not give its kWh rating or establish that it is part of the automatic island sequence.

technicalDetails.detail: Grid-connected services include frequency regulation, reactive-power and voltage support, peak shaving, demand response, and solar smoothing. During an upstream outage the point of common coupling opens and the battery, solar inverter, and available diesel generation support the station as a local island.

Duke reports the first automatic storm-driven transfer in April 2016, lasting 1 minute 15 seconds before grid return, followed by another successful automatic operation in September 2016. SEL says the system supports both manual and automatic operation; no comprehensive outage log or annual availability was published.

Duke placed the battery, transformers, distributed-energy switch, isolation recloser, controller, and communications on the utility side of the customer meter. This ownership model let the utility maintain the system without placing new operating responsibilities on the fire department.

The recovered legacy figure of 50 kW is the photovoltaic rating, not storage power, and a secondary claim of a 500 kW battery is inconsistent with the technical record. The supported battery values are 500 kWh and 200/240 kW. The 36-hour duration is an estimate; measured energy delivered, fuel savings, renewable share, and present battery health were not found.

sources.title: Implementing a Microgrid Using Standard Utility Control Equipment

fort-belvoir-dc-microgrid.summary: The legacy Fort Belvoir Direct Coupling DC Microgrid was a small Nextek-led 24 V DC building demonstration, not the later multi-megawatt Area 300 resilience microgrid. A 2022 national-laboratory survey describes the legacy work as a partial fluorescent-lighting retrofit with about 1 kW of DC load, direct-coupled photovoltaic generation, and no reported battery-backed islanding. Nextek's now-unavailable project page reportedly described 16 kW of solar, but that figure could not be independently confirmed from an accessible primary record. Fort Belvoir later completed a separate AC microgrid at Area 300 using 905 kW of fixed natural-gas generation and four 400 kW mobile diesel generators; those assets are documented here as a later project, not silently merged into the DC demonstration.
fort-belvoir-dc-microgrid.status: Historical demonstration with current operation unverified. The accessible national-laboratory survey characterizes the DC installation as a partial demonstration rather than a campus microgrid, and the original vendor page is no longer available. Separately, the Area 300 cybersecure AC microgrid completed a 72-hour demonstration on January 13–15, 2020, but the mobile generators are not permanently stationed there and the final report says full mobilization after an unplanned outage could take roughly 24–36 hours.

organizations.name: U.S. Army / Fort Belvoir
organizations.role: Host of both the legacy building-scale DC demonstration and the separate later Area 300 AC resilience project.

organizations.name: Nextek Power Systems
organizations.role: Integrator associated with the legacy direct-coupling DC lighting and photovoltaic demonstration.

organizations.name: S&C Electric Company
organizations.role: Prime contractor and system integrator for the separate Area 300 ESTCP microgrid, led by principal investigator Aura Lee Keating.

organizations.name: IPERC
organizations.role: Supplied the GridMaster distributed microgrid control platform for Area 300.

organizations.name: U.S. Army Combat Capabilities Development Command C5ISR Center NVESD
organizations.role: Partnered on control-network integration and cybersecurity for the Area 300 project.

organizations.name: Dominion Energy Virginia
organizations.role: Modified and coordinated the existing 34.5 kV distribution system used by the Area 300 island.

specifications.label: Legacy DC bus and load
specifications.value: 24 V DC with approximately 1 kW of fluorescent-lighting load in a partial building retrofit

specifications.label: Legacy photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: A historical Nextek project page reportedly listed 16 kW; the accessible LBNL survey confirms photovoltaic generation but does not provide that capacity, so 16 kW remains archive-only and independently unverified

specifications.label: Legacy storage and islanding
specifications.value: No battery storage or islanding capability is identified in the national-laboratory DC-building survey

specifications.label: Separate Area 300 fixed generation
specifications.value: Three natural-gas generators rated 205 kW, 325 kW, and 375 kW, totaling 905 kW

specifications.label: Separate Area 300 mobile generation
specifications.value: Four 249th Engineer Battalion 400 kW diesel generator sets, totaling 1.6 MW; combined fixed-plus-mobile capacity was 2.505 MW

specifications.label: Separate Area 300 service area
specifications.value: 13 buildings on an existing 34.5 kV distribution network, with ten more buildings served than the project's original resilience baseline

equipment.detail: Direct-coupled 24 V DC photovoltaic and lighting demonstration. Accessible records do not disclose converter, panel, luminaire, or protection-device models.
Distributed intelligent power controllers and a human-machine interface coordinated generation, feeder switching, black start, and resynchronization over a cybersecure network.
Generator controls interfaced the three existing natural-gas units with the GridMaster system.
Four 400 kW Army mobile diesel generators operated at 4.16 kV and connected to the 34.5 kV system through a 2 MVA transformer.
Managed the control network integrated into the installation's closed restricted network.
The project added reclosers, disconnects, grounding transformers, bypass contactors, and associated medium-voltage equipment to sectionalize and island the existing distribution system.

technicalDetails.detail: The recovered record's title points to Nextek's small direct-coupled DC project. The 2016–2021 ESTCP effort at Area 300 is a distinct 34.5 kV AC microgrid with different scale, assets, contractors, and purpose. Its capacities are therefore labeled separately rather than used to overwrite the legacy DC project's identity.

A 72-hour demonstration in January 2020 exercised islanding, black start, weekday mission loads, N+1 generation redundancy, and reconnection. Black start was completed in about 90 seconds, beating a less-than-five-minute objective.

The four mobile generators are shared Army assets rather than permanently installed capacity. The final report estimated typical utility coordination at 12–24 hours and complete asset mobilization at 24–36 hours after an unplanned event, so the demonstrated island was not continuously ready in its full test configuration.

The demonstrated generation mix was natural gas plus diesel. The project did not incorporate renewable generation or energy storage, and no fuel-consumption, annual island-hours, or emissions data were published.

The final report groups approximate costs as $500,000 for high-voltage work, $400,000 for low-voltage work, $50,000 for generator use and fuel, and $350,000 for controls, studies, and training. These are demonstration categories, not a verified lifecycle cost.

The legacy Nextek page is preserved only as an archive reference and its 16 kW claim lacks accessible independent verification. No current operating data, renewable fraction, energy savings, or proof of continued use was found for the DC installation. CERDEC tactical research located at Fort Belvoir is not attributed to this project merely because it shares the base.

Cybersecure Microgrid Control System at Fort Belvoir — Final Report

sources.title: S&C Brings Resilience to Fort Belvoir

fort-carson-microgrid.summary: Fort Carson's legacy microgrid record corresponds to Phase 2 of the Smart Power Infrastructure Demonstration for Energy Reliability and Security (SPIDERS), a three-feeder, seven-building cybersecure demonstration completed in 2013–2014. It integrated three existing diesel generators, part of a 2 MW photovoltaic plant, and five bidirectional electric-vehicle chargers. The 72-hour test succeeded, but a 2025 Pacific Northwest National Laboratory handbook, based on a December 3, 2024 installation interview, reports that proprietary controls were not sustained, had become obsolete and unsupported by about 2020, and left the system effectively inoperable or 'bricked.' Fort Carson subsequently installed a separate 4.25 MW/8.5 MWh lithium-ion battery in 2018–2019 and completed a separate 1 MW/10 MWh flow battery in November 2024; neither should be presented as proof that SPIDERS remains operational.
fort-carson-microgrid.status: Legacy SPIDERS microgrid is abandoned and effectively inoperable. It was handed to the installation without a durable operations-and-maintenance or firmware-support path; limited training and proprietary controls made revival too expensive relative to mission priority. Separate later assets remain: the lithium-ion demand-management BESS was implemented in January 2019, and the GridStar Flow long-duration demonstration finished construction in November 2024 and entered an 18-month test program. No public 2025–2026 operating results were located for the flow pilot.

organizations.name: U.S. Army / Fort Carson
organizations.role: Host and eventual owner of SPIDERS and host of the later lithium-ion and flow-battery projects.

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy
organizations.role: Led preliminary design and interagency support for SPIDERS Phase 2.

organizations.name: Burns & McDonnell
organizations.role: Performed SPIDERS detailed design, implementation, commissioning, testing, and training.

organizations.name: IPERC
organizations.role: Supplied the proprietary distributed microgrid control platform used by SPIDERS.

organizations.name: Lockheed Martin
organizations.role: Supplied the GridStar lithium-ion BESS and the separate GridStar Flow long-duration battery.

organizations.name: Geli
organizations.role: Provided predictive software controls for the separate 2019 lithium-ion BESS.

organizations.name: AECOM
organizations.role: Energy-services contractor for the 2019 battery project, implemented through an energy-savings performance contract.

organizations.name: U.S. Army Engineer Research and Development Center
organizations.role: Leads the separate GridStar Flow field demonstration and 18-month validation effort.

specifications.label: SPIDERS service area
specifications.value: Three distribution feeders serving seven mission buildings

specifications.label: SPIDERS dispatchable generation
specifications.value: Three existing diesel generators operated with N+1 redundancy; individual make, model, and kW ratings are not disclosed in the project report

specifications.label: SPIDERS photovoltaic resource
specifications.value: A nominal 1 MW segment of the installation's 2 MW solar array was incorporated into the architecture, but only about 500 kW was available during the demonstration

specifications.label: SPIDERS electric-vehicle interface
specifications.value: Five Coritech bidirectional chargers connected three Smith Electric trucks and two Boulder Electric Vehicles vehicles

specifications.label: Separate lithium-ion BESS
specifications.value: 4.25 MW / 8.5 MWh, equivalent to two hours at rated power; implemented in January 2019 after installation work in late 2018

specifications.label: Separate flow battery
specifications.value: 1 MW / 10 MWh Lockheed Martin GridStar Flow redox-flow system, designed for up to ten hours of support to selected facilities; construction completed November 2024

equipment.detail: Distributed intelligent controllers, master controller, and human-machine interface coordinated generators, feeder switching, photovoltaic production, and vehicle-to-grid assets. Proprietary firmware and discontinued vendor support later became the principal reason the system could not be economically restored.
Five bidirectional chargers interfaced three Smith Electric trucks and two Boulder Electric Vehicles vehicles; the report does not publish per-charger kW or usable vehicle-battery energy.

High-voltage switchgear, automatic-transfer-switch bypass breakers, synchronizers, motorized switches, and network-distribution-transfer equipment enabled the three-feeder island and grid reconnection. Final device manufacturers and models are not comprehensively identified.

A containerized 4.25 MW/8.5 MWh installation, described by USACE as more than twelve battery containers, used for peak-demand reduction and energy-cost management rather than as a continuation of SPIDERS.
Artificial-intelligence and predictive-dispatch software optimizes the battery around installation load and tariff peaks.
A 1 MW/10 MWh aqueous redox-flow battery installed as the Department of Defense's first megawatt-scale long-duration field demonstration of this technology.

technicalDetails.detail: The island was formed in roughly ten minutes; photovoltaic generation was brought in about five to ten minutes later, and a 72-hour exercise successfully demonstrated cybersecure islanding, multiple generation sources, N+1 operation, and reconnection.

The 2025 PNNL handbook says the demonstration was transferred without adequate sustainment funding, firmware updates, documentation, or operator training. By about 2020 the proprietary controls were obsolete and no longer vendor-supported; the installation described the system as effectively bricked and chose not to revive it.

DOE's 2014 SPIDERS report treats the 2 MW photovoltaic plant as an existing resource during the 2013–2014 demonstration, while PNNL's 2025 retrospective lists the 2 MW system as commissioned in 2016. The sources are not reconciled, so the discrepancy is retained rather than assigning a false single commissioning date.

The roughly $8 million system was delivered through an energy-savings performance contract with no up-front installation cost to the base. Project sources forecast about a 9% peak-demand reduction and roughly $500,000–$525,000 per year of savings under a 20-year guarantee; these are projections, not independently audited realized savings.

The GridStar Flow system is intended to charge from Fort Carson solar, reduce peaks, and provide limited-facility backup for as long as ten hours. ERDC began an 18-month operational test after November 2024 construction completion; no test results, availability, round-trip efficiency, or delivered backup events were found.

An April 2023 base-wide black-start exercise covering 93 facilities was a separate readiness event and is not evidence that the SPIDERS controller was restored. Likewise, Fort Carson's reported 12% renewable-electricity share in fiscal 2023 is installation-wide, not the energy mix of the abandoned microgrid.

SPIDERS sources do not publish measured diesel-fuel savings or renewable percentage, so generic expectations such as 30% fuel reduction are not treated as project performance. Later storage capacity is labeled separately and must not be added to the legacy SPIDERS configuration.

fort-devens.summary: The legacy Fort Devens microgrid record is best supported as a 2009–2010 net-zero and secure-energy planning effort, not a commissioned permanent microgrid. The Army Reserve asked FEMP and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory to assess net-zero electric and thermal operation, while Sandia developed a strategy for critical-load safe havens lasting at least two weeks. Because the three priority load clusters were nonadjacent, the study concluded that as many as three microgrids might be required. It screened photovoltaic, geothermal, hydro, natural-gas generation, batteries, and propane backup, but did not select final capacities or vendors. A separate 2012–2013 Smart and Green Energy (SAGE) tactical base-camp demonstration at Fort Devens did operate three 100 kW Caterpillar generators, 12.9 kW of PV, and roughly 250 kWh of AGM battery storage; that portable equipment moved to Fort Leonard Wood after testing and was not the permanent net-zero installation envisioned in 2010.
fort-devens.status: Planning/concept project with realization unverified. The 2010 PNNL report documents assessment, site barriers, and candidate technologies rather than construction or commissioning, and current Fort Devens public-works and sustainability materials reviewed do not establish that the proposed installation-scale microgrid was built. The separate SAGE base-camp demonstration ran from January 2012 through March 2013, completed stable field trials and fuel-reduction testing, and then transferred its equipment to the Contingency Base Integration Technology Evaluation Center at Fort Leonard Wood.

organizations.name: U.S. Army Reserve / Fort Devens
organizations.role: Host and sponsor of the legacy net-zero and critical-load microgrid planning effort and host of the separate base-camp demonstration.

organizations.name: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory
organizations.role: Performed the 2010 site assessment and led evaluation and reporting for the separate SAGE field demonstration.

organizations.name: Federal Energy Management Program
organizations.role: Supported the installation-wide net-zero assessment requested after the Army Reserve's April 2009 initiative.

organizations.name: Sandia National Laboratories
organizations.role: Developed the secure microgrid strategy and participated in Base Camp Integration Laboratory energy demonstrations.

organizations.name: MassDevelopment
organizations.role: Municipal-utility owner/operator context for much of the redeveloped former base, creating interconnection and wheeling considerations for the Army's noncontiguous parcels.

organizations.name: Caterpillar
organizations.role: Supplied the prime diesel-generator sets, controls, and bidirectional inverter used in the separate SAGE tactical demonstration.

organizations.name: LEX Products
organizations.role: Supplied the PowerHOUSE tactical power-distribution equipment for SAGE.

specifications.label: Legacy resilience objective
specifications.value: Critical-base-operation safe havens capable of operating for at least two weeks during a utility outage, alongside a longer-term net-zero electric and thermal goal

specifications.label: Legacy prospective topology
specifications.value: Three nonadjacent priority clusters—Maloney, Ear Muffs, and Barnum—making one contiguous island impractical and potentially requiring three microgrids

specifications.label: Legacy screened resources
specifications.value: Rooftop, parking-canopy, ground, and landfill photovoltaic generation; geothermal heat pumps; waste heat; nearby hydro; natural-gas fuel cells or microturbines; flow or conventional batteries; and propane backup—all planning candidates, not a final installed mix

specifications.label: Adjacent hydro opportunity
specifications.value: A privatized nearby hydro plant was estimated to provide about 250 kW of firm capacity with a possible 400 kW expansion; off-site procurement and wheeling barriers meant it was not established as a Fort Devens microgrid asset

specifications.label: Separate SAGE prime generation
specifications.value: Three Caterpillar XQ100 100 kW diesel/JP-8 generator sets, with an optional 60 kW MEP-806A tactical quiet generator

specifications.label: Separate SAGE photovoltaic capacity
specifications.value: Two Sharp photovoltaic arrays totaling 12.9 kW with Enphase microinverters

specifications.label: Separate SAGE storage
specifications.value: Approximately 250 kWh modeled usable capacity through a Caterpillar BDI-250 250 kW bidirectional inverter; designed for 100 kW for two hours or 250 kW for 20 minutes and laboratory-tested for 2 hours 14 minutes at 100 kW and 26 minutes at 250 kW

equipment.detail: The 2010 assessment considered photovoltaic arrays, geothermal wells and heat pumps, waste-heat recovery, hydro purchases, natural-gas fuel cells or microturbines, conventional or flow batteries, and propane backup. No final manufacturers, models, ratings, or procurement decisions were documented, so these are not treated as installed equipment.
Three 100 kW mobile diesel/JP-8 units were coordinated as a right-sized microgrid instead of the baseline configuration of six independently operated 60 kW tactical generators.
Mobile 208/120 V, three-phase distribution connected the generators, storage, photovoltaic generation, and base-camp loads.
A 250 kW bidirectional inverter charged and discharged the battery banks and supported silent or reduced-generator operation.

Four banks of 384 valve-regulated lead-acid AGM cells provided the demonstration's electrochemical storage. The report models approximately 250 kWh of capacity but does not present a simple manufacturer-rated nameplate kWh total.
Two Sharp module arrays with Enphase microinverters supplied 12.9 kW total.

Supervisory software dispatched generators and storage in response to load and resource conditions. The detailed report does not identify a separate commercial manufacturer for the DSOM implementation.

technicalDetails.detail: The 2010 work identified resource options, critical-load clusters, and regulatory and ownership barriers. It did not produce an as-built design. Some rooftop photovoltaic projects and geothermal wells already had appropriations, but their installed capacity and integration into a microgrid were not established in the reviewed report.

After the 1996 base realignment, the Army retained roughly 4,800 acres in noncontiguous areas while MassDevelopment operated the surrounding municipal utility and Wellesley operated distribution. State-law, interconnection, and power-wheeling constraints complicated use of off-site renewable resources across the installation.

The Base Camp Integration Laboratory opened in June 2011 with twin 150-soldier camps. SAGE testing ran from January 2012 through March 2013, comparing the optimized microgrid, photovoltaics, storage, and efficiency measures against six 60 kW tactical generators.

The field report found stable operation and fuel reductions up to 34% for the microgrid controls alone, 20.6% in one reported phase, 30.1% for a modeled/scaled combination of microgrid plus 52 kW of PV and storage, and as much as 54% when all tested technologies were combined. Photovoltaics supplied as much as 12% of daily energy. These figures refer to different test configurations and should not be collapsed into one annual result.

After the Fort Devens demonstration, the portable system was moved to Fort Leonard Wood's Contingency Base Integration Technology Evaluation Center for continued testing. It therefore should not be counted as permanent Fort Devens generation or storage.

No commissioning record, as-built capacity, vendor inventory, measured energy mix, or current operating data was found for the 2009–2010 installation-scale concept. A separate CERDEC claim of 37% fuel savings and the SAGE results may reflect different base-camp configurations and are not assigned to the permanent concept.
